author: Threch
title: Advanced Deep Learning course
comment_content:
A large part of content comes from Stanford's CS231n and Michigan's EECS 498-007 / 598-005. Professor will also share his research related to Computer Vision, which is impressive and inspiring.

Content:
1. Image classification
2. Loss function and optimization
3. Introduction to neural networks
4. Convolutional neural networks (CNN)
5. Training neural networks
6. Deep learning hardware and software
7. CNN architectures
8. Recurrent neural networks (RNN)
9. Attention and Transformers
10. Detection and segmentation
11. Generative models
12. Visualizing and understanding
13. Video Models
14. Self-supervised Learning
15. Deep 3D Vision
16. Learning-based Sensing Technology
17. Invertibility in Image Processing and Restoration
18. Other Research Review...

Very fruitful content and advanced knowledge involved.

Coursework:
1. Programming Assignments x3, each 12%
2. Midterm Exam, 35%
3. Project: 29%
- Proposal: 2%
- Milestone: 3%
- Presentation: 4%
- Report: 20%

Having late policy which allows you to share 8 late days for assignment and each up to 4 days. For group project, you can have a group up to 3 people and share another 8 late days in total.
comment_teaching:
During the lectures, cqf mainly just reads the slides and add some explanations to some complicated concepts. However, his voice is too soft and I can even hear better in zoom rather than sitting in the classroom, which is ironic. Also, his delivery skill is not good such that it is quite difficult to understand what he wants to present even the concepts or contents are easy.

There are some lecture videos on Youtube of CS231n. You may want to watch those videos to understand the major content of this course, which is recommended. The lecture slides are also mainly come from CS231n.

There are Lab session for helping students to understand the coding in deep learning and help them for finishing the assignments. You can watch the lab videos if you want on the course website and visit the related tutorials posted, although those hyperlinks are mostly outdated or 404 not found. D:

Assignments are copied from CS231n, you can find many useful reference on web. However, the assignments are using outdated version of Tensorflow and PyTorch (Tensorflow v1 D:). You may need to set up your own environment and even modify the code of utilities provided.

The research of cqf is inspiring and impressive, which may be favorable for those passionate in Computer Vision.
comment_grading:
Based on curve, generally the grading is good for me.

My score:
Assignment: Full for all (mean for each assignment is around 90-95 / 100)
Midterm: 74/100, mean 70.33
Project: Full for Proposal, Milestone and presentation (whether many students full for these parts also). 93/100 for final report  (mean is around 92.x)

Final grade: B+
comment_workload:
Average or High.

For those who are new to deep learning, you may need time to follow the contents and understand it. If you have the relevant knowledge on it, then the workload should not be high for you.

Assignment is not very difficult. You can refer to Lab assignment help session or online resources for helping you to finish it.

Midterm is quite difficult to me as I don't have any knowledge in machine learning before. It is open book so that you can view your notes on your notebook, which saves your time from reciting those concepts and architectures.

Project workload is varied and depended. You can choose any  topic you want related to Computer Vision. The most time-consuming part is the final report. It needs to be written in CVPR style for 6-8 pages.

title: Deep Learning in Stanford's CS231N
comment_content:
Contents were mostly directly imported from Stanford University's CS231N: Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition, with some small amount of additional topics from the instructor's own research.

The course goes into more depth in the Math behind Deep Learning (DL) when compared to COMP 4211 (Machine Learning). If you are more interested in the Math behind modern ML, then this is the better course for you. However, if you only want a broad overview of modern ML, COMP 4211 is a better choice.

At any rate, the contents were as follows:
Image classification
Regularization and optimization*
Neural Networks and Backpropagation*
Convolutional neural networks*
CNN architectures*
Training neural networks*
Recurrent neural networks*
Attention and Transformers*
Video Models
Detection and segmentation
Visualizing and understanding
==== Midterm Coverage Up to Here ======
Self-supervsied Learning
Generative models
PixelRNN/CNN
Variational Autoencoders*
Generative Adversarial Networks*
3D Vision
Normalizing Flow and Diffusion Model
Graph Neural Networks
Robot Learning
Generative Video Synthesis

Much of the slides were a direct copy of CS231N.

Though the contents were indeed useful and is close to the current state-of-the-art of Computer Vision, there was no incentive for you to actually learn the materials after the midterm unless you were interested in the content or will be doing research in the area in the future.

Topics covered by COMP 4211 are marked with a asterisk (*), but this course covered most of them in more mathematical detail than COMP 4211.
comment_teaching:
I only attended the first and the last lecture (for project presentation).

Qifeng's lessons were basically slide-reading and were not very interesting. While he was knowledgeable in the field (I found a few of his papers when searching for information for our project, and he did talk about some of his research in the later lectures), self-learning from either videos of CS231N or doing the assignments were much more effective ways to learn the content in my opinion.
comment_grading:
Grading was reasonably generous.

The grading scheme was as follows:
36% 3 Programming Assignments, 12% Each
29% Midterm
35% Project
- 2% Project Proposal
- 3% Milestone Report
- 4% Interim Presentation
- 20% Final Report

8 late days were allocated for the programming assignments as a whole, and 8 late days were allocated per group for every component of the project (for a total of 16). A maximum of 4 late days can be used on any given component.

I used 1 late day for PA1, 4 late days for PA2 and 3 late days for PA3. Our group allocated 4 late days to the project milestone and 4 late days to the final report.

Programming Assignments
======================
Continuing the trend, programming assignments were also directly copied from Stanford's CS231N. They were quite challenging to complete in general, and you should expect to spend at least two weeks on each assignment. Each assignment included both written inline questions (in the provided Jupyter notebooks) and (vectorized) Python code implementations.

PA1 was on K-Nearest Neighbour Classifier, Support Vector Machines, Softmax Classifer, Two-Layer Neural Networks and Image Feature Extraction.
PA2 was on Multi-Layer Fully-Connected Neural Networks, Batch and Layer Normalization, Dropout, Convolutional Neural Networks and PyTorch.
PA3 was on Image Captioning for RNNs and Transformers, Generative Adversarial Networks, and Self-Supervised Learning. Bonus tasks included a LSTM-based implementation of the RNN part.

The assignments were not on the same level of difficulty as COMP 4211 - while COMP 4211 PAs were mainly training you on the use of popular ML libraries, you are expected to implement many of the functions from these libraries from scratch with only NumPy. The entire PA1, the majority of PA2 and some of PA3 are done in this manner. Possibly the most challenging part was figuring out the (vectorized) implementations of backpropagation for each layer with NumPy only - often, derivation of the correct formulae took me more time than actually writing the code. The most difficult part for me was the implementation of convolutional and spatial group normalization backpropagation in PA2.

You are strongly advised to reserve sufficient time for each assignment. Though solutions can be found online, they are often extremely optimized (and thus hard to understand).

No statistics were released for each programming assignment.

Midterm
======================
Midterms were a mix of multi-answer MCQs, some short questions, and two long questions. MCQs were mostly trivia which can be found on the lecture notes, but some required understanding of the materials over direct copying. Short questions tested the hypothetical outputs of some of the ML models presented with slight variations. One long question was on receptive fields of CNNs, while the other was on gradient descent on K-Means.

The difficulty of the midterm mainly stemmed from two places: the short time limit (one lecture, or around 90 minutes) and the tricky MCQs. The midterm was open electronic notes (but no communication was allowed), so memorization was not a significant factor.

The midterm statistics was as follows:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
0/40/53.17/56/65/90/100

Final Project
======================
The project was spread over the entire semester with four graded components - a project proposal, a milestone report, an interim presentation and a final project report (with code).

The project proposal was a ~200 word short description of your project, in which you briefly outline the problem, dataset and proposed model. After you have confirmed the topic of your project, this should not take too long to complete. In our semester, the project proposal was due around mid-October.

The milestone report was 2-3 pages, and should cover your literature survey, technical approach (in greater detail than the project proposal) and preliminary results. It was due around early to mid-November.

The interim presentation was scheduled after the end of all of the taught topics, and was a 4-5 minute presentation on your current progress. The PowerPoint slides must be submitted in advance before the first groups present. The PPTs were due around mid- to late November.

The final report was supposed to be formatted with the given LaTeX template, and should be structured as a Computer Vision Conference-style paper. It (along with your project code) was due before the end of the study break.

Since our group did not have sufficient time to work on the project, our interim presentation and milestone report did not include any notable results. Much of the work was done around the end of the semester (see the Workload section).

The distributions for all the project components were not disclosed.

Overall Grading
======================
Since most of the statistics are missing (not good practice in my opinion), I have no basis for comparison with other courses. Scoring ~90 per project component, ~90 per PA and UQ for the midterm was sufficient for a B+ in my case.
comment_workload:
The workload is somehow on par with, or even higher than the COMP 4211 by DY.

Each PA took up all of two weeks (and some late days) to satisfactorily complete. As you were expected to both write Python to accomplish some of the tasks and to complete written questions, both derivation of the required formulae and actual implementation took significant amounts of time.

Since the midterm was open electronic notes, little time was spent on revising for it on my end.

A large proportion of the workload stemmed from the project. Our group did not have enough time during the semester to dedicate to the project, which caused us to defer much of the work until the study break. It took us around 5 full days to train the model, evaluate the results and write the report, and we ended up using 4 late days as well. Please do not defer the workload of the project to the end of the semester like us (i.e. do not deadline fight the project) for the sake of your own health and/or mental stability.

author: J Yang
title: In-depth DL course
comment_content:
A combination of Stanford's CS231n and Michigan's EECS498-007. The professor also shares some of his research results regarding computer vision.

Youtube has a collection of Stanford CS231n offered in Spring 2017, which I highly recommend watching while studying this course. The syllabus is basically the same.

Three Python programming assignments. Can be easily sourced from various Github pages. Coding from scratch may take days and requires proficiency in NumPy and PyTorch. Luckily you can learn these packages when doing homework with these references.

The midterm is conventional. No coding and mostly chain rule. I attach one of the past papers to give you an idea.

The project requires 1-3 people per group. It could be a realization of a conference paper, some experiments on a new dataset, etc. etc. At last, you need to write a research paper of 6-8 pages in CVPR style.
comment_teaching:
Professor Chen mostly reads the slides. But since the slides are of high quality, I think it's not a bit of a problem.

There is a lab session. TAs will go through homework and introduce some usage of PyTorch of Tensorflow. Totally optional.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:
Assignments: 3 * 12% = 36%
Midterm: 35%
Project:
--Proposal: 2%
--Milestone: 3%
--Presentation: 4%
--Report: 20%

There is a late policy in this course. Assignments share a total of 8 late days with up to 4 late days per assignment. Project share a total of 8 total days as well.

Curve grading but fair. Here are some statistics of me and my friends:
96/100: A+
94/100: A
92/100: A-
The bins seem tight, but since everyone is doing great in assignments and projects, the only difference lies in midterm. Get prepared!
comment_workload:
Average workload.

Each assignment takes less than a week to finish. However, more time is needed if no prior Numpy or Anaconda experience.

Midterm takes about a weekend to review.

Project workload largely depends on the topic your group chooses. I would say it's time-consuming to write 6-8 pages in CVPR style. Just go check one of the CVPR papers to get a grip of what that means.

author: C2x
title: I take this as my 1st ML Course
comment_content: Content is the complete copy from Stanford CS231N. Deserves an A because this is Stanford’s most updated CS231N stuffs. Honestly, it is really lazy but I don’t see the point not to use their version as they already invested that much of effort in building up such an even-beginner-feels-friendly-to-start deep learning course.
comment_teaching:
Morning 9 am lectures with cqf’s teaching style really demotivates me to the class. His teaching makes me sleepy.

For lazy dogs like me who is a beginner to ML (I did not even take any of the COMP2211/3211/4211), I self learnt the course with Stanford’s CS231N. Please use the earlier version of their recording (2017, public on YouTube, lectured by Serena & Justin Johnson) for first several sets of slides and for later parts, use the latest version (2025, public on YouTube, lectured by Justin Johnson et al)

No recording is provided. Copyright issue?
comment_grading:
The only differentiator is probably the midterm. This year extends exam coverage up to topics like GAN and transformers. Find some past paper from his published pdfs and from CS231N. But make sure you grasp some more technical maths and parameter stuffs in transformer parts too as I believe these paper do not cover that.

I got 60+ in midterm with the mean being 55, with full in my PAs, which yields a B+. The course is competitive as many final year UGs are enrolled and most likely they have solid ML fundamentals.
comment_workload:
3 PAs: Each PA involves 5+ ipynb with each having 10+ tasks. Quite difficult but many who took CS231N generously gives many hints and their work. But still, there are some frustrating parts that annoyed me a lot:
1. You need to modify some import sys part in your ipython for every starter code column or otherwise your code gives error. Ask your TA to give that code to you.
2. For 3rd PA, you even need to downgrade your PyTorch version to 2.6.0 or your error is super high since the seed generated is version-dependent.
3. Avoid typing other weird Unicode symbols directly to replace latex inline commands (e.g please use $\neq$ instead of typing a direct ≠), since you can get rendering error when you execute your submission code for PDF conversion.
Midterm: You need to self-learn the calculus of matrices, which is kinda annoying (maybe he taught, idk anyways). Also the MCs are multiple-answers, which are also bad and tricky.

Projects: I feel they grade your work with some 90+ marks arbitrarily and that can make little difference? Presentation I guess everyone gets full marks. For final report I crammd some 2 days before using up all my late days and got that mark.

author: lajoya
comment_teaching:
really make me sleepy, he may be a good researcher but teaching is definitely not his advantage

a easy win compared to...
comment_workload: its ok for me, he leaves us enough time

author: thehound
title: Good good grading, hea hea teaching
comment_content: You may refer to other reviews, it's bascially copied from stanford cs231n. Overall I really enjoyed the materials.
comment_teaching: Well, what can I say. He does not want to teach I would say, just watch stanford lectures which is available on youtube.
comment_grading:
The breakdown is subject to change as a whole and adjustments on a per-student basis in exceptional cases. This is the general breakdown we'll be using:

Three Programming Assignments: 36%
Midterm: 35%
Final Project: 29%
Programming assignments are done individually.

There will be three programming assignments.

Assignment #1:
Image Classification, kNN, SVM, Softmax, Neural Network
Assignment #2:
Fully-Connected Nets, Batch Normalization, Dropout, Convolutional Nets
Assignment #3:
Image Captioning, Network Visualization, Style Transfer, Generative Adversarial Networks
Exceptions will be given only in extreme circumstances and only in advance.

Qifeng is one of the most lenient graders I met in ust. I got only 2 marks above median for the midterm and almost full scores for the assignment (in which everybody should be getting almost full scores since you can get the solutions from github, and he is very generous grading the group project), results as a B+.
comment_workload: Since he's superleniently grading your group project and you can find all the assignment solutions from github, you only need to focus on preparing for the midterm exam.

author: XanderGe
comment_content: Covers a lot of the young history of computer vision and includes a lot of super recent topics that have emerged in the past few years in the second half of the course. Learned a lot of useful theory and practical applications! The first half of lectures is required content for the midterm, second half was conducted mostly by Prof. Chen's grad students surveying more recent developments and applications of computer vision. No attendance is taken.
comment_teaching: Great, informative lectures. Professor Chen is very knowledgeable and receptive to all sorts of questions!
comment_grading: You'll basically get 100 on all the assignments and the final project (as long as you put at least some effort in). Grading all comes down to performance on the midterm which typically has one or two hard questions that will determine how well you do. The mean for the midterm is typically around 50-60. Despite this, the professor actively tries to give as many people a good grade as possible.
comment_workload:
3 Programming Assignments (from Stanford cs231n, typically implementing different core components to vision models and applying it to a dataset like CIFAR10)
1 Midterm (Open Note, could use notes/materials on your computer and any other links on the course website)
1 Final Project (Any computer vision topic, groups of 2-3)

author: ccccdd
comment_content:
大部分內容同 stanford 嘅 cs231n 同埋 michgen 嘅 Deep Learning for Computer Vision 非常相似。呢兩科網上都有片睇。十分推薦想讀呢科嘅人去睇下自己仲唔仲意，或者當預習。最尾professor會介紹下佢自己個研究。所以內容會比果兩科多啲同新啲。

功課方面一共三份同 cs231n 差唔多嘅功課，網上好多 source。最後一份 project，最多3人一組。final report係論文格式，6-8 page，當鍛煉下寫paper。

一個 mid term 冇 final。多數學嘢，冇coding。mid term嘅source就好難搵，睇題目非常基礎，比如問下RNN入面gradient點計出來，計下CNN嘅output size。mid term算難。
comment_teaching: zoom上課就有啲悶。呢幾年網上嘅ML或者tensorflow嘅course都多，推薦唔明嘅時候搵搵呢啲。或者問下TA。
comment_grading: 整科 weighted total 有 91+ 有A。我估mid term同project係容易拉大差距嘅地方，整體grade算好。
comment_workload:
三份功課每份內容都多，就算睇source都要一段時間先睇得明，精通python有優勢。

三份功課共享8日delay 但每樣最多4日，通常後兩個會用到幾日。project嘅 proposal, milestone, final report都一樣8日delay，我就用晒去 milestone 同 final report。
